title: Fitting a door stop and weather seal
subtitle: Fix compressible draught strips and a threshold seal on an external door
excerpt: How to choose and fit the correct weather seal for each side and the foot of an external door: selecting between foam, rubber fin, wiper, and brush strip types, cutting to length, and securing each strip so it compresses evenly against the door without causing binding.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual.

glossaryTerms.definition: A compressible strip of foam, rubber, silicone fin, or brush pile that fits between the door edge and the frame to block the gap. The strip must compress enough to seal against the door face without causing the door to bind or the latch to fail to engage.

A seal fitted to the foot of an external door to close the gap between the door bottom and the sill. The most effective type for an external door is an automatic drop-seal that rises when the door opens and drops to contact the sill when the door closes.

content.text: The type of
draught excluder
matters: foam self-adhesive strips suit light interior use; rubber fin or silicone wiper seals withstand the pressure of a well-hung external door. At the foot, a
threshold seal
that automatically drops when the door closes is more effective than a static brush or foam strip that can compress permanently over time.
Step 1, strip old seals and prepare surfaces

Remove all old foam strips by peeling and then cleaning the adhesive residue with a solvent or scraper. Sand any rough edges on the door stop and frame face. Check the door hangs correctly: a sagging or binding door needs hinge adjustment before new seals are fitted, as the seal will be uneven in compression and fail quickly in the overloaded zone.
Step 2, choose the correct seal type for each edge
Hinge side:
Rubber or silicone wiper strip fitted to the door stop face. The hinge side has minimal clearance; a foam strip compresses permanently here within weeks.
Latch side:
Rubber fin or silicone wiper strip on the door stop. Must compress fully when the door closes but not resist the latch engaging.
Top edge:
Brush pile strip or foam strip on the top door stop face. Less pressure here; either type works.
Bottom edge:
Automatic drop-seal or wiper seal on the door foot. A static brush strip is the minimum; an automatic drop-seal is the most effective option.
Step 3, cut strips to length and fix

Cut all three head and side strips to length with a tenon saw. Self-adhesive versions: peel the backing and press onto a clean, dry surface. Pin-fixed versions: position the strip and fix with the 20 mm pins supplied at 200 mm (8 in) intervals. Butt the hinge-side strip to the floor and the head strip to the top of the hinge-side strip. The latch-side strip butts to the head strip.
Step 4, fit the threshold seal

For a screw-fixed wiper or brush strip on the door foot: hold the strip at the correct height (the seal should just contact the sill when the door is closed) and mark the screw holes. Drill pilot holes and fix. For an automatic drop-seal: fix the body to the door face or door foot as specified in the kit, set the trigger plate at the door stop face, and adjust the seal drop height so the seal clears the sill by 2 mm when the door is open and contacts it when closed.
Troubleshooter

attrs.symptom: Door binds and is hard to close after fitting seals
attrs.cause: The seal is compressing too much, or the seal on the latch side is preventing the latch from reaching the keep.
attrs.fix: Check each seal in turn: close the door against each edge and feel for resistance. Reduce the thickness of the seal on the binding side or reposition the strip further back from the door face.

attrs.symptom: Gap persists at the foot of the door even after fitting a threshold strip
attrs.cause: The sill is not level, or the door has dropped on its hinges so the bottom edge is not parallel to the sill.
attrs.fix: Check the door bottom edge is parallel to the sill with a long spirit level. Adjust hinge positions to re-level the door before relying on the threshold seal to bridge a tapered gap.
